bullet_points: Load the hayashi_grid_summary.csv and hayashi_grid.json files.
Print all CDM baseline values (f_cool=1.0, M_cut=0) of Nabs_total at each redshift (z=7, 8, 9, 10, 12, 15) alongside the corresponding Tk_cdm values.
Verify that the cumulvec arrays in the JSON are monotonically decreasing and span the expected tau range (~0.001 to ~0.5).
Save a summary of the validated data to a new file for subsequent steps.
Convert the cumulative distribution cumulvec into the differential distribution dN/dtau via finite differences on the log-spaced tau_arr (dN/dtau = -d(cumulvec)/dtau).
Compute the derivatives partial (dN/dtau) / partial M_cut and partial (dN/dtau) / partial f_cool using finite differences between adjacent grid points.
Print the ratio of the maximum absolute derivative values to quantify the dominance of structure suppression over cooling at each redshift and tau bin.
Save the differential distributions and derivative results to disk.
sub_tasks.sub_task: Sensitivity Analysis of the Forest Spectrum

bullet_points: Implement physical mapping functions to convert M_cut and f_cool into cross-section values sigma_0/m_chi using power-law interpolation in log-log space.
Incorporate the z-dependence of the relative velocity for the n=-4 (Coulomb-like) case, where v_rel ~ (1+z)^(1/2), and print the exact scaling relation used for each redshift.
Save the mapped cross-section data for Fisher analysis.
Perform a Fisher matrix analysis using the differential distribution dN/dtau to estimate the sensitivity of SKA-era observations to M_cut.
Assume a Poisson noise model sigma(dN/dtau) = sqrt(N_obs) / Delta_tau with N_sightlines = 10.
Compute Fisher information for both the total absorber count N_abs (scalar) and the full differential distribution dN/dtau (vector), and print the ratio of the two.
Calculate the 1-sigma and 2-sigma confidence intervals for sigma_0/m_chi at each redshift and save the results.

Generate multi-panel plots: (a) N(>tau) cumulative distributions for all M_cut at z=10; (b) dN/dtau curves for varying M_cut at z=10; (c) fractional change in N_abs vs z and M_cut; (d) Fisher-forecasted sensitivity contours in the sigma_0/m_chi vs. z plane; (e) Fisher information vs. redshift and signal-to-noise ratio per tau bin.
Identify the optimal redshift and propose an optimized tau-bin weighting scheme based on the Fisher analysis.
Save all plots as high-resolution PNG files.
Compile existing constraints from CMB (Xu et al. 2018), global 21 cm signal (EDGES/SARAS3), and Lyman-alpha forest data.
Quantitatively compare the 21 cm forest sensitivity (from Step 4) with existing bounds using the ratio sigma_forest / sigma_CMB.
Synthesize the findings to emphasize the unique sensitivity of the 21 cm forest to the structure suppression channel and discuss the impact of astrophysical nuisance parameters.

Write the full Results section of the paper, structured into: (1) baseline validation and CDM predictions, (2) differential spectrum sensitivity and dominance of structure suppression, (3) parameter mapping and cross-section sensitivity curves, (4) Fisher forecasts and optimal observational strategy, and (5) comparison with existing constraints.
Integrate all quantitative results, interpretations of the plots, and key statistics generated in previous steps, ensuring all numerical values are explicitly quoted with physical units.
